content.text: Non-blocking HTTP clients are the transport layer that enables async Python agents to issue dozens of concurrent network calls — to LLM providers, tool APIs, and databases — without stalling the event loop between them. The two dominant choices are
(battle-tested, websocket-capable). Understanding their lifecycle, connection pooling, timeout model, and streaming interface is prerequisite knowledge for building production-grade agentic systems.
-compatible methods for making HTTP requests. When a coroutine calls

, the event loop suspends that coroutine, hands the socket to the OS, and runs other ready coroutines until the response arrives. Control returns to the original coroutine without ever blocking a thread.
In Python the canonical async HTTP client is
API exactly, adds HTTP/2 support, and integrates natively with asyncio. A lower-level alternative is

, which predates httpx and remains widely used for websockets and high-throughput scenarios. Both maintain an internal connection pool that keeps TCP connections alive across awaits, avoiding the overhead of TLS handshakes on every request.
internally. Prefer them when calling a specific provider — they handle auth headers, retry logic, and streaming protocols. Use
directly when calling arbitrary REST endpoints from agent tool implementations.

Agent loops are I/O-heavy workloads. A single LLM request takes 1–30 seconds. A search tool call takes 200–800 ms. A database gateway call takes 10–100 ms. If each of these is issued sequentially with a synchronous HTTP client, the total wall time for one agent turn is the
and async clients, the total wall time is the
The second concern is the event loop. FastAPI, Starlette, and similar frameworks run request handlers as asyncio coroutines. A blocking synchronous call inside an async handler — such as

— seizes the OS thread and prevents the event loop from advancing any other coroutine while waiting. Under a 5 s LLM call this means 500+ concurrent sessions on the same worker are frozen. Async clients release the loop on every

Streaming token delivery depends on async clients as well. LLM providers emit Server-Sent Events (SSE) one token at a time. Only an async streaming client can forward each chunk to the browser as it arrives without buffering the entire response first.
fans out dozens of HTTP calls; the event loop manages all of them on a single thread.
— the internal pool keeps TCP/TLS connections alive across awaits, cutting per-request overhead from ~50 ms (TLS handshake) to ~1 ms (reuse).
— httpx AsyncClient with HTTP/2 enabled can send multiple requests over one TCP connection, further reducing overhead for burst tool calls.
exactly, so the migration path from sync code is mechanical.
separates connect, read, write, and pool timeouts. LLM reads legitimately take 120 s; tool connects should fail at 5 s. Fine-grained control prevents one slow service from blocking others.
context. It cannot be called from synchronous code without wrapping in
— creating a new client per request exhausts file descriptors (each client holds sockets in the pool). A single application-scoped client must be created at startup and explicitly closed at shutdown.
by default raises the first exception encountered. Passing
is required when some tool calls are expected to fail without aborting the entire batch.
— async stack traces are harder to read. A timeout that fires deep in an awaited chain may surface at an unexpected call site.
— async HTTP clients are for I/O concurrency, not CPU parallelism. CPU-heavy post-processing of responses still blocks the loop; use
or process pools for that work.
At the OS level, async HTTP clients use
. When a write to a socket would block (send buffer full) or a read finds no data yet, the kernel returns immediately with
instead of suspending the thread. The event loop registers a callback on the socket's file descriptor with
(macOS/BSD). When the OS signals that data is available, the loop fires the callback, which resumes the awaiting coroutine.
Both httpx and aiohttp maintain a pool of keep-alive TCP connections indexed by
, the client checks whether an idle connection to
exists in the pool. If so, it reuses it immediately, skipping TCP and TLS setup entirely. If not, it opens a new connection up to the pool's configured

. For agent services calling one LLM endpoint and several tool endpoints concurrently, keeping defaults is typically safe. High-throughput services should tune these to match the concurrency level and the upstream server's connection limits.
— time to establish a TCP connection (default: 5 s).
— time between receiving bytes from the response body (default: 5 s). For LLM streaming, set this to 120 s or
— time to flush the request body (default: 5 s).
— time to wait for a free connection from the pool (default: 5 s). Setting this prevents indefinite waits when all pool slots are busy.
applies the same value to all four fields. For production agents, explicitly separate LLM timeouts (long read) from tool timeouts (short connect + read).
When a server sends a chunked or SSE response, the HTTP client does not buffer the entire body before returning. Instead,
returns an async context manager. Inside it,
are async generators that yield each chunk as it arrives from the OS socket buffer. Each
— the event loop can service other coroutines between chunks.
The diagram below shows how an agent handler, the asyncio event loop, an httpx.AsyncClient, and three external services interact during a single agent turn that issues parallel HTTP calls.

attrs.alt: Async HTTP Client Architecture — event loop, connection pool, and parallel fan-out to LLM API, Tool API, and Database API

content.text: . Each coroutine executes until its first

, at which point it suspends. The event loop registers the three socket file descriptors and waits for any of them to become readable. As each response arrives, the corresponding coroutine resumes. All three calls overlap in wall time; the handler resumes when the last one completes.
Building async agent servers (FastAPI, Starlette, Quart) that call external APIs.
Issuing parallel tool calls inside an agent turn using
Consuming streaming LLM responses (SSE) and forwarding tokens incrementally to clients.
Calling arbitrary REST endpoints that have no official async SDK.
Use sync httpx.Client or requests when:
Running in synchronous CLI scripts, Celery tasks, or worker thread contexts.
Using a framework that is not async (Django views without ASGI, Flask without eventlet).
Prototyping where async overhead is not worth the complexity.
Prefer official async SDKs over raw httpx when:
— it handles auth, retries, and streaming protocol details.
— similarly wraps httpx and manages API-specific semantics.
The SDK exists and is maintained — it reduces boilerplate and is tested against the API.
The standard pattern for an async agent server is to create one shared
at application startup and inject it into route handlers via the app's state object.
Step 1: Create the client in a FastAPI lifespan context

import httpx
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    limits = httpx.Limits(
        max_connections=100,
        max_keepalive_connections=20,
        keepalive_expiry=30.0,
    )
    # Long read timeout for LLM calls; short connect for quick failure detection
    timeout = httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=120.0, write=10.0, pool=5.0)
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient(
        limits=limits,
        timeout=timeout,
        http2=True,               # enable HTTP/2 when the server supports it
    )
    yield
    await app.state.http.aclose()   # always close to flush the pool

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
Step 2: Access the client in route handlers

@app.post("/agent/run")
async def run_agent(request: Request, body: dict):
    client: httpx.AsyncClient = request.app.state.http

    # Single awaited call — suspends this handler, event loop runs others
    resp = await client.post(
        "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
        json=body,
        headers={"x-api-key": API_KEY, "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()
Step 3: Fan out parallel tool calls

import asyncio

async def call_tools_in_parallel(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    tool_calls: list[dict],
) -> list[dict]:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)   # cap concurrent upstream requests

    async def one(tc: dict) -> dict:
        async with sem:
            resp = await client.post(
                f"http://tools-gateway/{tc['name']}",
                json=tc["input"],
                timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=2.0, read=30.0),
            )
            resp.raise_for_status()
            return {"tool_use_id": tc["id"], "content": resp.json()}

    results = await asyncio.gather(
        *[one(tc) for tc in tool_calls],
        return_exceptions=True,   # don't abort if one tool fails
    )

    # Normalise exceptions into error results
    out = []
    for tc, result in zip(tool_calls, results):
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            out.append({
                "tool_use_id": tc["id"],
                "is_error": True,
                "content": str(result),
            })
        else:
            out.append(result)
    return out
A self-contained script that fetches JSON from a public endpoint. Run with

import asyncio
import httpx

async def fetch_json(url: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch a JSON endpoint and return the parsed response."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        response = await client.get(url)
        response.raise_for_status()   # raises httpx.HTTPStatusError on 4xx/5xx
        return response.json()

async def main():
    data = await fetch_json("https://httpbin.org/json")
    print(data["slideshow"]["title"])   # "Sample Slide Show"

    # Parallel: fetch three endpoints concurrently
    urls = [
        "https://httpbin.org/get",
        "https://httpbin.org/headers",
        "https://httpbin.org/user-agent",
    ]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[fetch_json(u) for u in urls])
    for r in results:
        print(r)

asyncio.run(main())
Example 2 — Streaming SSE tokens from an LLM
This example streams tokens from an Anthropic-compatible SSE endpoint and prints each token as it arrives, demonstrating how

import asyncio
import json
import httpx

ANTHROPIC_URL = "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages"

async def stream_tokens(api_key: str, prompt: str) -> None:
    """Print tokens from a Claude response as they stream in."""
    payload = {
        "model": "claude-opus-4-5",
        "max_tokens": 256,
        "stream": True,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    }
    headers = {
        "x-api-key": api_key,
        "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01",
        "content-type": "application/json",
    }
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=120.0)) as client:
        async with client.stream("POST", ANTHROPIC_URL, json=payload, headers=headers) as resp:
            resp.raise_for_status()
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if not line.startswith("data: "):
                    continue
                data_str = line[len("data: "):]
                if data_str == "[DONE]":
                    break
                chunk = json.loads(data_str)
                if chunk.get("type") == "content_block_delta":
                    delta = chunk.get("delta", {}).get("text", "")
                    print(delta, end="", flush=True)
    print()   # newline after stream ends

# asyncio.run(stream_tokens("sk-ant-...", "Write a haiku about async I/O."))

Consider a production agent that processes customer queries. Each turn the model emits 3–5 tool calls simultaneously: a vector search call, a CRM lookup, and a pricing API query. Issuing these sequentially would take 300 ms + 150 ms + 100 ms = 550 ms. With
, the wall time is max(300, 150, 100) = 300 ms — a 45% latency reduction on every agent turn, compounding over multi-turn conversations.

import asyncio
import httpx
import json

async def execute_agent_turn(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    tool_calls: list[dict],
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Execute all tool_calls from a model response in parallel.
    Returns tool_result blocks ready to send back to the model.
    """
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)

    async def run_tool(tc: dict) -> dict:
        async with sem:
            try:
                resp = await client.post(
                    f"http://tools-gateway/{tc['name']}",
                    json=tc["input"],
                    timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=2.0, read=45.0),
                )
                resp.raise_for_status()
                return {
                    "type": "tool_result",
                    "tool_use_id": tc["id"],
                    "content": json.dumps(resp.json()),
                }
            except httpx.TimeoutException:
                return {
                    "type": "tool_result",
                    "tool_use_id": tc["id"],
                    "is_error": True,
                    "content": f"Tool '{tc['name']}' timed out after 45 s",
                }
            except httpx.HTTPStatusError as exc:
                return {
                    "type": "tool_result",
                    "tool_use_id": tc["id"],
                    "is_error": True,
                    "content": f"HTTP {exc.response.status_code}: {exc.response.text[:300]}",
                }

    return list(await asyncio.gather(*[run_tool(tc) for tc in tool_calls]))

At scale (1,000 concurrent agent sessions on 4 uvicorn workers), this pattern allows each worker's single event loop thread to handle hundreds of overlapping tool calls without spawning additional threads or processes. The I/O workload is cooperative and transparent to the OS thread scheduler.
Mistake 1 — Creating a new client per request

inside a route handler opens a new connection pool on every request. Under load this exhausts the OS's file descriptor limit. Each client also performs a new TLS handshake per call, adding 50–200 ms overhead.
Mistake 2 — Using a sync HTTP call inside an async handler
handler blocks the OS thread for the entire duration of the call, freezing every other session on that worker.
Mistake 3 — Not handling exceptions in gather
raises the first exception and cancels remaining tasks. Forgetting
means one failing tool aborts the entire batch, dropping results from all other successful calls.

# WRONG: first exception aborts all remaining tool calls
results = await asyncio.gather(*coros)   # raises on first failure

# RIGHT: collect both successes and failures
results = await asyncio.gather(*coros, return_exceptions=True)
tool_results = [
    handle_error(tc, r) if isinstance(r, Exception) else r
    for tc, r in zip(tool_calls, results)
]
Mistake 4 — Applying a single timeout to LLM and tool calls

LLM responses legitimately take 20–120 s for long generations. Tool calls should fail fast at 5–30 s. Using one global timeout either kills valid LLM responses early or allows tool calls to hang indefinitely.

# WRONG: one global timeout fits neither LLM nor tool correctly
client = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0)

# RIGHT: override per-request
llm_resp = await client.post(LLM_URL, json=payload,
                              timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=120.0))
tool_resp = await client.post(TOOL_URL, json=tool_input,
                               timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=2.0, read=15.0))
One client per application, not per request.
Create the client in the app's lifespan context, store it in
with separate connect and read values. Never omit timeouts in production — a hung upstream server will hold a pool slot forever.
to prevent stampeding an upstream service with more concurrent connections than its rate limit allows.
Process failures per-tool rather than aborting the entire batch. Return structured error tool_results so the model can decide how to proceed.
Prefer official async SDKs for provider APIs.
They handle retries, exponential back-off, and API versioning headers correctly.
Enable HTTP/2 when the server supports it.
— it multiplexes multiple requests over one TCP connection, reducing overhead for bursts of small requests to the same host.
Wrap entire agent turns with a deadline so runaway loops do not hang indefinitely, independent of per-request timeouts.
The theoretical throughput gain from parallel async requests is bounded by the slowest call in each gather batch. For an agent turn with N tool calls of duration
, sequential execution takes sum(d_i) while parallel execution takes max(d_i). The speedup factor is sum(d_i) / max(d_i), which grows with N and with variance in latencies.
Connection pool sizing affects throughput directly. If
but 50 coroutines all try to issue requests simultaneously, 30 coroutines wait up to
seconds for a free slot. For agent servers handling 200+ concurrent sessions, set
based on the upstream server's connection limit divided by the number of workers, leaving headroom for spikes.

HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces the need for large pools when calling one host. With HTTP/2, multiple in-flight requests share one TCP connection, each as an independent stream. This is especially effective for LLM APIs that use HTTP/2 — a pool of 5–10 connections can service 100+ concurrent streams.
For CPU-bound post-processing (embedding normalization, JSON parsing of large payloads, regex extraction), off-load to
. Async HTTP clients remove I/O blocking but do not help with CPU blocking.
When benchmarking async vs sync HTTP:
Compare with identical upstream latencies — mock servers like
Measure at realistic concurrency (e.g., 50 simultaneous agent turns) to reveal pool exhaustion and contention effects that don't appear in single-request tests.
The primary async HTTP client for Python. Install:
to enable HTTP/2. The httpx ecosystem includes
for mocking HTTP calls in tests.
Older, battle-tested alternative. Does not support HTTP/2. Preferred when WebSocket connections or very high throughput (> 10k req/s) are required. Install:

import aiohttp

async def fetch_with_aiohttp(url: str) -> dict:
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=30)) as resp:
            resp.raise_for_status()
            return await resp.json()
Prefer official async SDKs over raw httpx for LLM provider calls:

import anthropic

# AsyncAnthropic wraps httpx.AsyncClient internally
client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")

async def call_claude(prompt: str) -> str:
    message = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text

# Streaming with the SDK
async def stream_claude(prompt: str):
    async with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            yield text
— httpx mock library for unit testing async HTTP calls without network access.
— marks test functions as async and runs them under asyncio.
— low-level transport override for custom response stubs.
Legacy; blocks event loop if used in async
Calling Anthropic Claude APIs; best option for that use-case
Migration path when refactoring sync code
httpx verifies TLS certificates by default using the system CA bundle (via
in production — it disables certificate validation and exposes the agent to MITM attacks. If calling internal services with private CAs, pass
API keys are typically passed as Authorization or custom headers. Store them in environment variables, not in source code. Pass them via
on the client or per-request. httpx logs request and response details at DEBUG level — disable that logging or filter Authorization headers in production to prevent key leakage in logs.

import os
import httpx
import logging

# Suppress httpx's verbose transport logging in production
logging.getLogger("httpx").setLevel(logging.WARNING)

# Read API keys from environment, never hardcode
client = httpx.AsyncClient(
    headers={"x-api-key": os.environ["TOOL_API_KEY"]},
    verify=True,   # default; explicit for clarity
)
httpx does not retry by default. For agent tool calls, implement explicit retry logic around transient failures (
, HTTP 429, HTTP 503). Use exponential back-off with jitter to avoid thundering herd when many agent sessions retry simultaneously.

import asyncio
import random
import httpx

async def fetch_with_retry(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    url: str,
    *,
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> httpx.Response:
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            resp = await client.get(url)
            if resp.status_code == 429:
                retry_after = float(resp.headers.get("retry-after", 1.0))
                await asyncio.sleep(retry_after + random.uniform(0, 0.5))
                continue
            resp.raise_for_status()
            return resp
        except (httpx.ConnectError, httpx.TimeoutException) as exc:
            if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                raise
            backoff = (2 ** attempt) + random.uniform(0, 0.5)
            await asyncio.sleep(backoff)
    raise RuntimeError("Unreachable")
LLM APIs impose per-minute token and request limits. When running many agent sessions concurrently, a shared
or a token-bucket implementation can throttle outgoing requests to stay within provider limits. Exceeding limits causes 429 errors that, if not handled correctly, cascade into failed agent turns.
Use httpx.AsyncClient (or official async SDKs) in all async agent code.
handler — it blocks the event loop.
One client per application, closed on shutdown.
Create the client in the app's lifespan context, inject via
to drain the connection pool cleanly.
asyncio.gather() with return_exceptions=True for parallel tool calls.
Total agent turn latency drops from the sum of tool latencies to the maximum. Always handle individual tool failures without aborting the batch.
Set separate connect and read timeouts per call type.
LLM reads need 120 s or more; tool calls should fail at 5–30 s. A single global timeout suits neither.
Prevents stampeding upstream services when hundreds of agent sessions fan out simultaneously.
Use client.stream() with aiter_lines() for SSE token delivery.
iteration is an await — the event loop services other sessions between token chunks.
TLS verification must remain enabled in production.
Mock HTTP calls in tests with respx or aioresponses.
Tests should not make real network calls — mock at the transport layer for deterministic, fast unit tests of async agent code.
